EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on 15 western Washington rivers, with eight river forecast points reaching major flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East of Enumclaw, a mountainside gave way and covered both lanes of State Route 410.  The slide was about 300 feet long.  Landslides in the vicinity of a bridge west of Enumclaw along State Route 169 resulted in the bridge's closure until the following summer when repair work can be done.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain led to flooding across portions of Chelan County. The Stehekin River at Stehekin exceeded the flood stage of 24 feet, peaking at 24.1 feet.  Residences reported some water over Stehekin Valley Road near High Bridge.  The White River near Lake Wenatchee also flooded a portion of White River Road.  Nearby, the town of Plain recorded 4.12 inches of rain in a 24 hour period.
Heavy rain caused flooding on 15 western Washington rivers, with eight river forecast points reaching major flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Softened ground from heavy rain combined with strong winds blew down several trees, and their dislodged root balls collapsed an earthen section of the Mill Creek dam near Cosmopolis.  A footbridge was knocked out and some nearby residential areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to central and eastern Washington.  A front tracked through late on the 20th into the 21st, followed by a cold low pressure system over the area.  Numerous collisions and slide-offs were reported in the Spokane and Ritzville areas.  Storm total snow accumulations from this storm ranged mostly from five to 10 inches.  The higher accumulations of ten inches were recorded three miles west of Chelan, four miles north of Winona and six miles south-southeast of St. John on the palouse, and eight miles north-northwest of Rockford in Spokane county.

Another strong Pacific system brought more snow to the already snow-laden southwest Washington Cascades. This storm was particularly strong and very cold, resulting in low elevation snow as well, with snow accumulations reported in the Columbia River Gorge. Multiple school closures occurred and were contributed to hazardous road conditions after snowfall blanketed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty inches accumulated snowfall was reported in Skamania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Directly after a strong Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the region, a second equally strong system dumped more snow over the southwest Washington Cascades and Foothills. An additional two to three feet of snow blanketed the Cascades, with areas in the Foothills gaining up to a foot. This storm would be the second of three that would significantly increase the snow pack throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches new snowfall was recorded in the hills of Brush Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stubblefield Fire, centered 14 miles northeast of Bridgeport, had an unknown cause and burned 1,500 acres.
The Badger Mountain Complex Fire, centered ten miles southwest of Waterville, had an unknown cause, and burned 15,023 acres.
The #243 Fire, centered eight miles east of Desert Aire, was a human caused fire that burned 1,384 acres.
The Stuhlmiller Fire, centered seven miles west of Quincy, had an unknown cause and burned 1,200 acres.
The Green Lake Fire, centered five miles west of Omak, had an unknown cause and burned 2,614 acres.  The fire burned into the month of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wellpinit Complex Fire, centered 16 miles north of Davenport, was a human caused fire that burned 785 acres.

Brief heavy rain led to flash flooding near Pullman and 14 miles east of Pomeroy.  Five to six miles north to northeast of Pullman, flooding was observed on Estes and Old Ambion roads.  Fourteen miles east of Pomeroy, 1.6 inches of rain was measured in 20 minutes near Alpowa Summit on Highway 12.  This led to several road closures on the southwest side of the summit.
A severe thunderstorm dumped quarter sized hail in Dishman.
The Smith Lake Fire, centered four miles west of Grand Coulee, was a fire of unknown cause and burned 12,513 acres.
The Malott Fire, centered two miles northwest of Malott, was a lightning caused fire that burned 333 acres.
The Green Lake Fire, centered five miles west of Omak, burned 2,614 acres.  The fire start date was July 31st.

Heavy rain fell in the Methow Valley with mud and rock descending down the steep ridges on to Highway 20.  Highway 20 was closed just past its junction with Highway 153.  Nearby weather stations recorded near one inch of rainfall in three hours.

Under a transitional upper level ridge of high pressure and with the influence of the surface thermal trough along the coast, good east winds set up through the Columbia River Gorge and over the Cascades into the Foothills.  Though not widespread, a few locations recorded strong winds under this pattern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust was recorded at the Larch Mountain RAWS.  Time of observation is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low tracked across the northern Washington Cascades, followed by an arctic front passage.  On the Washington Palouse, one indirect fatality was contributed to icy road conditions on Highway 195 near Albion Road.  Drifting snow and low visibility led to multiple slide-offs and the closing of State Route 27 from Tekoa to Fairfield.  Numerous other accidents resulted from the first significant snow of the season for most areas.  Snow accumulations from the storm ranged mainly from five to nine inches, but with higher amounts in the Okanogan Highlands and Northeast Mountains.  In the Okanogan Highlands, spotters three miles west of Boyds and eight miles southwest of Inchelium picked up a foot of snow.  In the Northeast Mountains, a foot was also reported nine miles north of Metaline Falls.

An area of low pressure tracked across southeast Washington into north Idaho, with heavy snow on the north and west side of this storm.  This resulted in heavy snow accumulations to the Spokane area, northeast Mountains, and Okanogan Highlands.  The snowfall event added to the snow load on roof tops, with several major roof collapses occurring the rest of the month and into the first part of January in the Spokane area.  By the end of December, at least 19 roof collapses were reported, with two direct injuries from falling debris and snow.  Many businesses in the Spokane area closed to take preventative measurable for removing snow.  The snow also contributed to natural gas leaks as snow or ice sheared off gas meters. Several flights were cancelled for both arrivals and departures at Spokane International Airport.  Several slide-offs also occurred on Interstate 90.  The Spokane area as well as Deer Park received the heaviest accumulations from this storm, ranging from nine to 11 inches.  Other accumulations include eight inches in Inchelium, seven inches in Springdale, and four inches in Republic.  A couple hundred Deer Park residents lost power as melting snow downed some power lines.  This storm put Spokane over the top with the snowiest month since records began in 1893.  A total of 61.5 inches of snow fell in Spokane in December 2008.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near stationary arctic front boundary resulted in significant snow accumulations with record snow amounts reached in the Spokane area.  From 10 AM on the 17th through 10 AM on the 18th, Spokane Airport measured 19.4 inches of snow, breaking the previous 24 hour record of 13 inches.  The heavy snow closed roads, schools, businesses, Spokane County Courts, and contributed to numerous car accidents.  On the 17th, the Washington State Patrol responded to about 200 wrecks in Eastern Washington.  One such accident blocked interstate 90 in Spokane.  Bus service was also temporarily suspended with garbage collection also cancelled for a day.  Numerous flights were also either delays or cancelled flying out of Spokane International Airport.  The heavy snow put Spokane street plows into a condition red.  Private contractors were brought in to help clear snow from streets.  Portions of State Routes 27, 194, 195, and 274 were closed in Eastern Washington.  Total snow accumulations in the Spokane area ranged from 19 to 25 inches with the highest accumulations on the south and east end.  Other areas received heavy snowfall as well.  On the Washington Palouse; Oakesdale measured 24 inches, Colfax 16 inches, 14 inches four miles west-southwest of Palouse, Pullman 12 inches, and La Crosse eight inches.  In the Northeast Washington Mountains; 21 inches was measured seven miles south-southwest of Newport, 19 inches five miles southwest of Usk, 17 inches in Deer Park, 17 inches five miles northeast of Elk, 16 inches in Clayton, 15 inches three miles north-northeast of Loon Lake, and seven inches five miles south of Northport.  In the Okanogan Highlands, seven inches was recorded four miles west of Boyds.  In lower Garfield and Asotin counties, a very impressive 20 inches fell in Pomeroy.  In the northeast Blue Mountains, Ski Bluewood picked up 25 inches, while only six inches four miles west of Anatone.  The Wenatchee area, Waterville Plateau, and Upper Columbia Basin picked up four to six inches.

A strong warm front brought heavy snow to much of Central and Eastern Washington.  On the Palouse, gusty winds also contributed to poor visibility with blowing and drifting snow.  This led to the closure of State routes 272 and 27.  In Spokane, the weight of snow on roofs started to become a problem.  After the snow had ended a woman from Rockford, 20 miles southeast of Spokane, suffocated to death after snow had apparently fell from her rooftop and buried her while shoveling snow.  The weight of the snow at a hotel caused three historic ceiling tiles to crash to the lobby floor.  A business in the Spokane Valley experienced a roof collapse.  After this storm Spokane County declared a state of emergency.  The Palouse also began to see roof collapses, with a couple reported in Colfax.  Storm total snow accumulations ranged from four to eight inches with locally higher amounts.  Some of the higher totals include 12 inches 15 miles north-northwest of Leavenworth.  Ten inches were reported in Rosalia, seven miles south of Liberty Lake, and four miles west of Anatone.

An area of low pressure tracked across southeast Washington into north Idaho, with heavy snow on the north and west side of this storm.  This resulted in heavy snow accumulations to the Spokane area, northeast Mountains, and Okanogan Highlands.  The snowfall event added to the snow load on roof tops, with several major roof collapses occurring the rest of the month and into the first part of January in the Spokane area.  By the end of December, at least 19 roof collapses were reported, with two direct injuries from falling debris and snow.  Many businesses in the Spokane area closed to take preventative measurable for removing snow.  The snow also contributed to natural gas leaks as snow or ice sheared off gas meters. Several flights were cancelled for both arrivals and departures at Spokane International Airport.  Several slide-offs also occurred on Interstate 90.  The Spokane area as well as Deer Park received the heaviest accumulations from this storm, ranging from nine to 11 inches.  Other accumulations include eight inches in Inchelium, seven inches in Springdale, and four inches in Republic.  A couple hundred Deer Park residents lost power as melting snow downed some power lines.  This storm put Spokane over the top with the snowiest month since records began in 1893.  A total of 61.5 inches of snow fell in Spokane in December 2008.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to central and eastern Washington.  A front tracked through late on the 20th into the 21st, followed by a cold low pressure system over the area.  Numerous collisions and slide-offs were reported in the Spokane and Ritzville areas.  Storm total snow accumulations from this storm ranged mostly from five to 10 inches.  The higher accumulations of ten inches were recorded three miles west of Chelan, four miles north of Winona and six miles south-southeast of St. John on the palouse, and eight miles north-northwest of Rockford in Spokane county.

A strong warm front followed by the passage of an upper level low resulted in heavy snow across much of Eastern Washington.  Snow accumulations ranged between five to eight inches across most locations, with up to four inches in the Upper Columbia Basin.  Snow amounts include 8.5 inches in Clayton, five to eight inches in the Spokane area, and seven inches in Pullman.  Over 200 customers lost power in the Spokane area from falling debris onto power lines.  School delays and closures were common from the storm. A multiple-car crash on Highway 291 just north of Spokane resulted in five indirect injuries and a temporarily closing of the highway.  T.J. Meenach Drive was also closed in Spokane for a few hours from cars sliding off the road.  Snow and drifting snow contributed to a temporary closing of State Routes 272, 194 and 72 in Whitman County, with highway 26 partially blocked seven miles west of Colfax due to multiple slide-offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a rapidly advancing cold front, a severe thunderstorm moved through Clark county in southwest Washington and spawned a tornado.  The tornado touched down near Vancouver Lake, moved east into Hazel Dell, then continued eastward to Hockinson while experiencing multiple touchdowns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado had estimated wind speeds of 90 to 110 mph. Damage reports along the path of the tornado include: 200+ trees down, most between 12 to 24 inches in diameter, and approximately 6 trees greater than 36 inches; 30 to 40 homes damaged, with primarily roof damage; 19 power poles were snapped off; 3 lightly constructed structures were destroyed; a parked semi-trailer was tipped over, and several dozen sculling boats were damaged near Vancouver Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist low pressure system brought heavy snow to the North Panhandle.  Mount Spokane picked up nine inches of snow.  In the valleys, six inches was measured near Clayton, and five inches in Springdale.

This system brought the first surge of cold air south from western Canada accompanied by the first snow event during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 4 inches of snow fell, with 8 inches the highest report from a weather spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passage brought a period of brief heavy snow and gusty winds creating blowing snow between 8 PM to midnight on the 14th.  Sustained winds around 25 to 30 mph with gusts to 45 MPH behind the cold front resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow through the early morning hours on the 15th.  An estimated three inches of accumulation occurred from this system.  U.S. Highway 195 from Pullman to the Idaho Border was closed during this event from the blowing and drifting snow.  Most schools in the Pullman area were closed or delayed.  Several thousand homes and businesses lost power because of the storm.  Conditions were not as bad across the lower elevations of Whitman County given slightly warmer temperatures.

A winter storm brought significant snow accumulations to central and eastern Washington.  A front tracked through late on the 20th into the 21st, followed by a cold low pressure system over the area.  Numerous collisions and slide-offs were reported in the Spokane and Ritzville areas.  Storm total snow accumulations from this storm ranged mostly from five to 10 inches.  The higher accumulations of ten inches were recorded three miles west of Chelan, four miles north of Winona and six miles south-southeast of St. John on the palouse, and eight miles north-northwest of Rockford in Spokane county.

Strong wind gusts were measured on the Washington Palouse with Uniontown recording a wind gust of 60 miles per hour and Pullman 59 miles per hour.  In Clarkston, the high winds blew down several trees and power lines.  The wind also caused minor damage to a business under construction.

The third in a series of an unusually cold storm systems brought heavy snow accumulations to southwest Washington.  The heavy snowfall created a significant impact to many communities across southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 20 and 40 inches of snow fell in the south Washington Cascade foothills based on a few spotter reports and similar reports from the north Oregon Cascade Foothills.

A deep low pressure system produced damaging winds across extreme southeast Washington. Wind damage was nearly 5 million dollars in Walla Walla County, especially in the Walla Walla and College Place areas. There were high profile vehicles blown over, trees toppled, power outages, and roofs damaged. Wind gusts included 78 mph at the Walla Walla airport.

A deep low pressure system tracked towards Vancouver Island, with strong upslope flow into the East Slopes of the North Washington Cascades.  This resulted in heavy snow with accumulations ranging from six to 11 inches.  Amounts include 11 inches in Twisp, 8.5 inches in Winthrop, eight inches in Conconully, and six inches in Carlton.
A cold upper trough resided over the Inland Northwest where heavy snow fell across the Northeast Washington Mountains.  Snow accumulations include six inches in Colville and Northport.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 1st, snow depth at Snoqualmie Pass was 130 inches, or 165 percent of normal.  On Friday, Feb. 8th, all 3 passes - Snoqualmie, Stevens and White, were all closed, the first time this had happened in 11 years.  While the snowfall did not cause any damage, the Washington DOT's snow removal budget was already over budget by $4.6 million.  Stevens Pass ski area suspended operations for Friday and Saturday since no one could get there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 10 inches of snow, plus some-what warm and windy conditions, created avalanche hazards on Snoqualmie, Stevens and White passes.  The three day total snowfall at Snoqualmie Pass was 28 inches, at Stevens Pass 34 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow and considerable blowing snow to portions of northeast Washington.  Numerous road closured occurred during and after the storm.  Highway two from Wilbur to Creston was closed.  In southern and western Spokane county, all county roads were closed on February 8th because of high snow drifts after the storm.  In the Spokane and Pullman areas, snow ended between 3-4 AM, but considerable blowing snow occurred through the morning.  At Spokane International, a peak wind gust of 52 MPH was measured at 5:02 AM.  In Pullman, a peak wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded at 5:06 AM.  In Clarkston, the Asotin County Library was closed after a combination of ongoing maintenance and strong winds caused some sheetrock to crack. Snow accumulations from this event ranged mostly from four to six inches.

An avalanche destroyed a two-story vacation home built in 2007 near Lake Wenatchee.

A strong winter storm brought heavy snow and areas of blowing snow to portions of Eastern Washington.  Snow accumulations ranged mainly between four to seven inches.  The west plains area of Spokane was hit hard by strong winds and blowing snow during the early morning hours of the 31st with brief but occasional blizzard conditions between 2 AM and 4 AM.  This resulted in the closure of highway two just west of Airway Heights on Highway two.  Significant blowing and drifting snow was also reported on the Palouse.  A trained spotter five miles southwest of Anatone picked up the most snow with ten inches of accumulation.  After this storm the state governor of Washington declared a state of emergency for several counties, including Spokane and Whitman.  Numerous road and school closures resulted from the storm.  For the first time since 1985 Washington State University in Pullman was closed on the 31st and remained closed on February 1st.  Transit buses in Pullman also stopped running.

A slow moving frontal boundary resulted in heavy snow across much of Eastern Washington.  The frontal boundary lifted north over Spokane county on the 26th with primarily rain and significant mountain snow observed south of this boundary over southeast Washington.  A trained spotter in the northeast Blue Mountains four miles west of Anatone measured 19 inches of snow, with other reports of 15 to 17 inches.  Very significant valley accumulations occurred north of the frontal boundary over north Spokane County including the higher elevations such as Airway Heights and South Hill, extending north to the northeast Washington Mountains.   On South Hill in Spokane 17 inches of snow was measured.  The National Weather Service Office in Spokane picked up 15 inches of accumulation, with 13 inches at Spokane International Airport.  Mount Spokane ski resort was hit especially hard with 30 inches of accumulation.  Most other locations in the northeast Washington Mountains received between 12 to 18 inches.  The Davenport area in the Upper Columbia Basin received more than a foot of snow. Accumulations on the Washington Palouse ranged from six to nine inches.  Numerous road and school closures as well as power outages resulted from the storm.  Highway 195 from Pullman to the Idaho State Line closed on the 29th and remained closed until February 4th as additional storms brought more snowfall and blowing snow. State Route 272 which connects Colfax to the Palouse, also closed on the 29th and did not reopen until February 5th. These closures resulted in stranded residents in Colton and Uniontown. In Spokane, public schools closed for the first time since 1996.  Spokane City Hall closed on the 28th for the first time since 1980.  Eastern Washington University in Cheney also was closed on the 28th and 29th.

After this storm the state governor of Washington declared a state of emergency for several counties, including Spokane and Whitman.  Numerous road and school closures resulted from the storm.  For the first time since 1985 Washington State University in Pullman was closed on the 31st and remained closed on February 1st.  Transit buses in Pullman also stopped running.

A slow moving frontal boundary resulted in heavy snow across much of Eastern Washington.  The frontal boundary lifted north over Spokane county on the 26th with primarily rain and significant mountain snow observed south of this boundary over southeast Washington.  A trained spotter in the northeast Blue Mountains four miles west of Anatone measured 19 inches of snow, with other reports of 15 to 17 inches.  Very significant valley accumulations occurred north of the frontal boundary over north Spokane County including the higher elevations such as Airway Heights and South Hill, extending north to the northeast Washington Mountains.   On South Hill in Spokane 17 inches of snow was measured.  The National Weather Service Office in Spokane picked up 15 inches of accumulation, with 13 inches at Spokane International Airport.  Mount Spokane ski resort was hit especially hard with 30 inches of accumulation.  Most other locations in the northeast Washington Mountains received between 12 to 18 inches.  The Davenport area in the Upper Columbia Basin received more than a foot of snow. Accumulations on the Washington Palouse ranged from six to nine inches.  Numerous road and school closures as well as power outages resulted from the storm.  Highway 195 from Pullman to the Idaho State Line closed on the 29th and remained closed until February 4th as additional storms brought more snowfall and blowing snow. State Route 272 which connects Colfax to the Palouse, also closed on the 29th and did not reopen until February 5th. These closures resulted in stranded residents in Colton and Uniontown. In Spokane, public schools closed for the first time since 1996.  Spokane City Hall closed on the 28th for the first time since 1980.  Eastern Washington University in Cheney also was closed on the 28th and 29th.
Moist upslope flow brought heavy snowfall into the Blue Mountains. Snowfall amounts in inches included Ski Bluewood (13) and Touchet SnoTel (8.2).
A strong and very cold Pacific system brought heavy snow accumulations to southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 to 20 inches of snow fell in the south Washington Cascades Foothills based on similar reports from the north Oregon Cascade Foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 1st, snow depth at Snoqualmie Pass was 130 inches, or 165 percent of normal.  On Friday, Feb. 8th, all 3 passes - Snoqualmie, Stevens and White, were all closed, the first time this had happened in 11 years.  While the snowfall did not cause any damage, the Washington DOT's snow removal budget was already over budget by $4.6 million.  Stevens Pass ski area suspended operations for Friday and Saturday since no one could get there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurrican Ridge got 20 inches in 24 hrs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow and high winds to the area which caused blizzard conditions in the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 24 and 43 inches total snowfall accumulation fell, based on reports from sites around Mt St Helens such as June Lake, Sheep Canyon, Swift Creek, Lone Pine and Spencer Meadows.  Fifty to 70 mph winds were also reported in the south Washington Cascades causing white out conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front brought heavy snow to the northeast Washington Mountains.  The snow began during the early morning hours on the 21st  before ending by early afternoon.  Clayton and Springdale picked up six inches of accumulation, with four inches reported in Chewelah.

An unseasonably cold low pressure system tracked through the area resulting in widespread, heavy snow accumulations across much of northern Washington. Accumulations in the valleys were generally in the four to eight inch range, including four to six inches in the Spokane area, with local amounts of ten to 18 inches in the higher mountains near and above 4500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing morning temperatures from April 19th through April 22nd resulted extensive crop damage.  In the Wenatchee area, there was an estimated loss of 90 million dollars to the cherry crop, and 15 million to the pear crop.  The coldest morning was April 21st where the Wenatchee Airport reported a low of 25 degrees Fahrenheit.
Southerly flow brought moist and unstable air into eastern Washington. An upper level disturbance combined with daytime heating developed numerous severe thunderstorms.

Unseasonably warm temperatures led to significant mountain snow melt.  This led to a section of a road washing out along Highway 206 in Mount Spokane State Park.  This resulted from a creek which overwhelmed a culvert pipe, undercutting a large section of asphalt. The exact start and end times of this event are estimated. Meanwhile, minor flooding resulted on rivers in central and eastern Washington.  In Eastern Washington, The Spokane River at Spokane crested at 28.4 feet at 12:00 PM PST on May 23rd, where the flood stage is 27.0 feet.  The Kettle River at Ferry crested at 18.67 feet at 8:00 PM PST on May 19th, where the flood stage is 18.5 feet.  Near the Cascades, the Stehekin River at Stehekin crested at 25.64 feet at 1:45 AM PST on May 18th, where the flood stage is 24.0 feet.  The Methow River at Pateros crested at 10.21 feet at 11:45 AM PST on May 19th, where the flood stage is 10.0 feet.

Heavy rain from two thunderstorms led to flash flooding just west of Inchelium.  The heavy rain led to mud and rock slides on Bridge Creek Road near the Bridge Creek Summit.  Doppler radar indicated more than 2.5 inches of rain occurred in this area in less than two hours.

A large landslide sent a 100 foot wide swath of rock and mud down the north face of the Icicle Valley about 15 miles west of Leavenworth.  This caused a temporary diversion of Doctor Creek, with boulders, trees, and mud spilling into Icicle Creek.  Icicle Road was severely damaged and closed as a result at Ida Creek Campground.  The creek was already swollen to near flood stage by unseasonably warm temperatures and snow melt.  The estimated cost to build a new road where the slide occurred is one million dollars.  A bridge was also damaged by the slide with an estimated damage amount of $40K.

An upper level ridge and thermal trough across the Pacific Northwest produced temperatures above 100 degrees for two consecutive days. Temperatures on June 29 and 30, respectively, included Ellensburg (105, 106), Hanford (106, 107), Pasco (105, 104), Yakima (101, 102), Walla Walla (105, 101), Kennewick (102, 102), Sunnyside (105, 103), Whitman Mission (103, 100).

Unseasonably warm temperatures led to significant mountain snow melt.  This led to minor flooding on the Okanogan River at Tonasket. The river crested at 15.1 feet at 5:00 AM PST on May 22nd, where the flood stage is 15.0 feet.

Estimated winds of 60-70 mph from a severe thunderstorm took an entire wall off a six year old barn.  Damage extended at least three trestles into the barn and stripped aluminum siding and shingles from the barn.  Other damage on the ranch included six by six posts broken in half and the top of a tree estimated to be five inches in diameter was completely sheared off.

Unseasonably warm temperatures in late May led to significant mountain snow melt with flooding continuing on the Spokane River at the start of June.  The Spokane River at Spokane rose to the flood stage level of 27.0 feet at 8:30 AM PST on May 20th, crested at 28.4 feet at 12:00 PM PST on May 23rd, and then fell to below flood stage on June 5th.

Western Washington had its first hot spell of the year that followed below normal spring temperatures. So the heat was quite a sharp contrast. In addition, the heat helped push mountain snow melt streams higher (below flood levels) with swift running and cold water temperatures. This combination led to people seeking relief from the heat by heading to these swift running streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two indirect fatalities  due to the heat. Both are missing and likely drowned. One kayaker spilled into the Green River and disappeared.  One of 4 rafters, who where thrown into the Green River when their raft flipped, disappeared (King County).  The Green river was well below flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific system brought more snow to the already snow-laden southwest Washington Cascades. This storm was particularly strong and very cold, resulting in low elevation snow as well, with snow accumulations reported in the Columbia River Gorge. Multiple school closures occurred and were contributed to hazardous road conditions after snowfall blanketed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 17 and 20 inches accumulated snowfall was reported by SNOTEL stations on Mt. St. Helens, such as Spencer Meadows, Sheep Canyon, and June Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man who intended to spend only the day hiking on the slopes of Mount Rainier, died after being caught in winter-like weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man who intended to spend only the day hiking on the slopes of Mount Rainier, died after being caught in winter-like weather.  The man was unconscious when they found him and died after they brought him back to shelter at Camp Muir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm moved over the forecast area from off the Pacific, bringing heavy snow amounts to portions of southwest Washington. Heavy amounts were seen in the higher elevations, with some lower elevations even seeing trace amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches total accumulated snowfall was reported above Longview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively late-season winter storm brought heavy snow amounts to the the Cascades of southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-six inches total accumulated snowfall fell at the Sheep Canyon SNOTEL site; 26 inches at Lone Pine and 19 inches at June Lake.  All sites are on the south side of Mt. St. Helens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A January type storm hit western Washington with strong winds and 2 funnel clouds. Winds gusting to 45 mph knocked out power to nearly 35,000 customers. One fallen tree struck three mobile homes in Purdy.  South Kitsap high school students got the day off because of a power outage at the school.  The final evening run of the Port Townsend - Keystone ferry was canceled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reached about half way toward the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 13 year old girl died in an avalanche near Verlot. Strong winds accompanied this weather event in portions of western Washington as well along with heavy snow in the Olympics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts to 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just north of the Mount Baker ski area, 2 snowmobilers died and 1 was injured in an avalanche. Strong winds accompanied this weather event in portions of western Washington as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a gust to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold Pacific system brought copious amounts of new snow accumulations to the higher elevations of southwest Washington. This system dropped snow levels considerably, leading to the first snow accumulation of the year in areas within the Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 22 inches total accumulated snowfall fell, based on reports from several SNOTEL sites on Mt. St. Helens, such as June Lake, Sheep Canyon, Swift Creek, and Lone Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Directly after a strong Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the region, a second equally strong system dumped more snow over the southwest Washington Cascades and Foothills. An additional two to three feet of snow blanketed the Cascades, with areas in the Foothills gaining up to a foot. This storm would be the second of three that would significantly increase the snow pack throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 25 and 40 inches new snowfall was recorded by various SNOTEL stations on Mt. St. Helens, such as June Lake, Swift Creek, Lone Pine, and Sheep Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought large hail and wind damage to portions of Okanogan County.  The largest hail occurred 3.7 miles SSW of Disautel, where golf ball sized hail damaged a fire truck.  In addition, a 70 MPH wind gust was estimated just north of Oroville near the Canadian Border.

A round of severe thunderstorms occurred over portions of central and eastern Washington.  Hail up to one inch in diameter as well as 58 MPH wind gusts were observed from the thunderstorms.  The exception to this was a thunderstorm in Douglas county where hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was recorded.  The gusty winds from the thunderstorms knocked down tree branches as well as a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail cracked a windshield of a vehicle driving on U.S. Highway two.

The exception to this was a thunderstorm in Douglas county where hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was recorded.  The gusty winds from the thunderstorms knocked down tree branches as well as a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Steamboat Rock State Park, a tree was blown over.  Several other trees had five inch limbs broken.

The Jackass Butte Fire, centered five miles southwest of Omak, was a lightning caused fire that burned 1,000 acres.  Gusty and erratic winds resulted in rapid fire spread shortly after the begin time of the event.  Three outbuildings were also destroyed by the fire.  A casino and hotel were evacuated during the fire, with 15 homes threatened as well.  Six power poles also burned resulting in the use of battery power to keep dispatch and 911 functioning.  A transmission structure was also lost during the blaze resulting in power outages in Twisp, Winthrop, Malott, and South Okanogan.  Property damage from the fire was estimated to be $20,000.
The Rattlesnake Point Fire, centered one mile southwest of Malott, was a lightning caused fire that burned 2,625 acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of rain was also recorded in one hour.

A fire, started earlier in the week by a lightning strike, smoldered until weather conditions were conducive to set it loose. The fire quickly spread to nearly 8,000 acres. Although the fire was not a threat to homes or property, it did do substantial timber damage within the Gifford Pinchot National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire covered nearly 8,000 acres, located about 11 miles north of Trout Lake, and spanned between both Skamania and Yakima counties in the Gifford Pinchot National Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Willow Creek Fire, centered five miles east of Ephrata, had an unknown cause and burned 3,600 acres.  Three out buildings were destroyed in the fire.
The #262 Fire, centered ten miles south of Moses Lake, was human caused and burned 6,054 acres.  The fire burned near O'Sullivan Dam and Potholes Reservoir.
The Seep Lakes wildfire, centered four miles west of Warden, was a human caused fire that burned 1,950 acres.
The Seep Lakes wildfire, centered four miles west of Warden, was a human caused fire that burned 1,950 acres.  The fire started on June 29th.

A strong dry cold front dropped south from British Columbia during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Windy conditions and low humidities both ahead of and immediately behind the cold front resulted in new large fires over portions of the inland northwest.  The Spokane Valley Fire was human caused and destroyed 11 homes and 10 out buildings in the Dishman Hills area and burned 1,006 acres.  Conditions were very extreme when the fire started with Felts Field at 4 PM PST reported a temperature of 81 degrees, a relative humidity of 10 percent, and sustained west winds of 24 MPH gusting to 44 MPH.  A separate fire in Cheney destroyed a garage.  A state of emergency was declared by the state governor in Spokane and Ferry counties.  The strongest winds from this front channeled down the Okanogan Valley, where Oroville received a peak wind gust of 74 MPH, and Omak 59 MPH.  Numerous trees and power poles were blown down by the high winds.  There was a total of 5,000 power outages mostly around Oroville, Tonasket, and Omak.  In Tonasket, a ten inch diameter tree was downed by the winds, causing extensive damage to a carport and RV.  Power poles were also blown down by the winds in Tonasket.  Blowing dust was also common with the front passage with visibilites as low as a half mile in Omak.  Colville and areas just north of Colville also saw strong winds with gusts to 55 mph which resulted in numerous 12 inch diameter trees as well as several power lines being knocked down.  Eight thousand customers lost power in Colville.  Several trees were blown down on highway 20 west of Republic.  In Kettle Falls, wind gusts to 55 mph knocked down several tree branches. Several other large fires resulted which spread quickly by the strong winds.  These include the French Valley Fire, centered seven miles southeast of Omak, which burned 1,420 acres.  This fire had an unknown cause, which spread rapidly to the south on the 10th by strong north winds.  Fires in Stevens and Ferry Counties resulted mostly from downed power lines.  There was structural damage from the fires to a barn, shed, and home.

The Cayuse Fire, centered 12 miles northeast of Tonasket, had an unknown cause and burned 1,778 acres.  One resident lost about 25 vehicles that was parked around his home.  Eight homes on East Cayuse Mountain Road were evacuated.

The Doyle Complex Fire, centered six miles NNW of Boyds, was a human caused fire that burned 964 acres.  High winds on July 10th spread the fires and destroyed two homes and threatened 50 others near the community of Barstow.

A severe thunderstorm moved south from 12 miles northeast of Mead through Spokane Valley to Spokane's south hill area.  The thunderstorm dumped large hail ranging in size from a penny to a quarter.  Although when the storm was north of Spokane, 12 miles northeast of Mead, a public report of hail estimated to be golfball-sized in diameter was reported.  Near Millwood, hail covered a deck to a depth of three inches. Very heavy rain also occurred with the thunderstorm with streets, backyards, and a basement flooded.

A very unusual late season snowfall occurred on the Washington Palouse from Tekoa south to Uniontown. Pullman recorded 1.5 inches of accumulation.  This was the latest measurable snowfall in Pullman since records began in 1940. In fact, no snow had ever been reported during the month of June before this date. The snow was in response to a vigorous upper level low pressure system that delivered a blast of cold air from the northwest.  The high temperature in Pullman on this day was a chilly 48 degrees.  The weight of the wet snow damaged several trees in Colfax.  In Pullman, several downed power lines and broken tree branches resulted from the snow.  Property damage from this event is estimated at $2,000.
A severe thunderstorm resulted in penny to quarter sized hail in Asotin and Whitman counties.  Strong wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were also observed in portions of Asotin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten 18 to 24 inch diameter trees were uprooted by winds estimated between 50 to 60 miles per hour along Highway 129 between mile posts 6 and 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm resulted in penny to quarter sized hail in Asotin and Whitman counties.  Strong wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were also observed in portions of Asotin county.
Outflow winds from a thunderstorm caused damage to several trees and knocked down power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was killed after a tree fell on him during a thunderstorm.  The man was trying to keep a dock from floating away during the high winds.  The exact age of the man is not known, but was in his late 50s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jack Creek Fire, centered 13 miles southwest of Leavenworth, was a lightning caused fire that burned 1,058 acres.  Rain fell on the 19th which very little to no extra acres burned thereafter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail fell on Little Goose Dam Road.

Several four inch diameter tree limbs snapped as well.  Heavy rain of 1.46 in 30 minutes also trigger a mudslide of three feet deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees were downed by strong wind gusts from a thunderstorm.  The wind also damaged and blew away yard objects.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge and dry air brought excessive heat into eastern Washington.  Some locations which experienced multiple days of at least 100 degree temperatures included Dallesport (107, 109, 102), Ellensburg (102, 105, 106), Hanford (104, 106, 111), Pasco (103, 104, 103), Yakima (101, 101, 103), Walla Walla (103, 106, 108), Kennewick (103, 103, 104), Selah (100, 102, 102), Satus Pass (100, 100), Sunnyside (102, 105, 106), Whitman Mission (105, 104, 106), Goldendale (100, 103, 106), Trout Lake (100, 101), Moxee (100, 101), and Selah (100, 102, 102).

A flash flood occurred just west of Lake Wenatchee due to heavy rain associated with thunderstorms.  The Viewpoint RAWS weather station was in the flash flood area where 2.12 inches of rain fell in four hours, with 1.18 inches falling between 3:04 PM PST to 4:04 PM PST.  Water was reported over White River Road two and a half miles west of Lake Wenatchee and over Highway two near Berne.
Outflow winds from a thunderstorm caused damage to several trees and knocked down power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top 40 feet of a 14 inch diameter tree was toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from a thunderstorm caused damage to several trees and knocked down power lines.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SOUTHERN CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge and dry air brought excessive heat into eastern Washington.  Some locations which experienced multiple days of at least 100 degree temperatures included Dallesport (107, 109, 102), Ellensburg (102, 105, 106), Hanford (104, 106, 111), Pasco (103, 104, 103), Yakima (101, 101, 103), Walla Walla (103, 106, 108), Kennewick (103, 103, 104), Selah (100, 102, 102), Satus Pass (100, 100), Sunnyside (102, 105, 106), Whitman Mission (105, 104, 106), Goldendale (100, 103, 106), Trout Lake (100, 101), Moxee (100, 101), and Selah (100, 102, 102).

A wildfire was started by lightning in north Whitman County near Rock Lake, burning more than 2,200 acres.  A heavy rain shower following the fire start slowed down the fire spread.
Two funnel clouds were spotted.  One over Lake Washington, and another near Black Diamond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Appeared over Lake Washington, on the northwest side of Mercer Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Swanson Lake Fire, centered 17 miles west of Davenport, was a fire of unknown cause and burned 19,090 acres.  The fire caused a brief closure of Highway two from Wilbur to Davenport.  Smoke blowing across State Route 25 caused a temporary closure from Davenport north to the Columbia River.
The Columbia River Road fire, centered nine miles west of Nespelem, was a fire of unknown cause and burned 22,155 acres.  Twenty homes and 40 outbuildings were threatened by the fire.

Brief heavy rain led to flash flooding near Pullman and 14 miles east of Pomeroy.  Five to six miles north to northeast of Pullman, flooding was observed on Estes and Old Ambion roads.  Fourteen miles east of Pomeroy, 1.6 inches of rain was measured in 20 minutes near Alpowa Summit on Highway 12.  This led to several road closures on the southwest side of the summit.

A severe thunderstorm moved south from 12 miles northeast of Mead through Spokane Valley to Spokane's south hill area.  The thunderstorm dumped large hail ranging in size from a penny to a quarter.  Although when the storm was north of Spokane, 12 miles northeast of Mead, a public report of hail estimated to be golfball-sized in diameter was reported.  Near Millwood, hail covered a deck to a depth of three inches. Very heavy rain also occurred with the thunderstorm with streets, backyards, and a basement flooded.

Very heavy rain led to flooding across portions of Chelan County. The Stehekin River at Stehekin exceeded the flood stage of 24 feet, peaking at 24.1 feet.  Residences reported some water over Stehekin Valley Road near High Bridge.  The White River near Lake Wenatchee also flooded a portion of White River Road.  Nearby, the town of Plain recorded 4.12 inches of rain in a 24 hour period.
